frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment: It's sad to see how many young married women today avoid wearing a mangalsutra or sindoor. These aren't just ornaments; they are symbols of our parampara and suhaag. A married woman truly looks complete and 'sobhagyavati' when she embraces these traditions. It's a matter of cultural pride and respect for our values.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment expresses disapproval when a married woman does not wear visible markers of marriage (mangalsutra, sindoor), framing it as a lack of completeness ('sobhagyavati') and disrespect for tradition ('parampara'). It subtly pressures women to conform to patriarchal marital norms under the guise of cultural pride, encoding the belief that a woman's identity is 'complete' only with these markers.
original_frame: Dress Code: Traditional Equal Respectable, Other Equal Questionable.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 45. Married. A Brahmin man in Pune. You are commenting under your real name on a news article shared on Facebook about a college in Maharashtra that introduced a dress code banning jeans and sleeveless tops for female students. Most commenters are criticising the college. You are writing a comment defending the dress code and explaining why you think institutions have the right and responsibility to regulate how young women dress and what happens in societies where there are no such standards.
